ITEMS.title: 3rd largest crude draw in history: 16.15M bbls total
ITEMS.summary: US crude inventories fell 16.15M barrels — the third-largest weekly draw on record. EIA confirmed a 4.49M bbl draw; implied demand rose to 21.1M bpd. HFI interprets this as bullish for oil prices and flags it as an unusual signal that markets may be underpricing supply tightness.
ITEMS.tweetA: 3rd largest crude draw in history: –16.15M bbls total (EIA: –4.49M). Implied demand hit 21.1M bpd. Oil markets may be badly mispricing supply tightness right now. $USO $XLE
ITEMS.tweetB: HFI Research: EIA crude draw of –4.49M bbls; total implied draw 16.15M — 3rd largest ever. Demand at 21.1M bpd. Bullish for oil. Hard to square with the consensus 'demand is weak' narrative.

ITEMS.title: ISM 54.0 = pull-forward demand, not organic recovery
ITEMS.summary: Peter Boockvar flags May ISM Manufacturing at 54.0 as misleading. The orders surge reflects front-running of tariffs, not genuine demand. He points to the 'Customers' Inventories' component plunging to multi-year lows as evidence buyers are stocking up ahead of price hikes. He is bearish on the durability of this print.
ITEMS.tweetA: ISM Manufacturing hit 54.0 — but Boockvar says this is tariff front-running, not a real recovery. Customers' Inventories collapsed to multi-year lows. When the pull-forward ends, so does the pop. $SPY
ITEMS.tweetB: Peter Boockvar: ISM 54.0 is a mirage. Buyers stocking up ahead of tariffs explains the new orders surge. The inventory drawdown confirms it. Don't mistake supply-chain gaming for organic growth.

ITEMS.title: ISM 54.0 — employment contracting for 32nd straight month while orders boom
ITEMS.summary: Neil Sethi's ISM breakdown highlights a structural contradiction: the employment sub-index has now contracted for 32 consecutive months even as new orders soar. This divergence suggests manufacturers are fulfilling orders with existing capacity, not hiring. Sethi treats this as a sign of post-pandemic productive efficiency, but flags it as a potential labour-market warning.
ITEMS.tweetA: ISM 54.0, but manufacturing employment has contracted 32 months in a row. Orders boom, no one's being hired. Is this permanent efficiency — or a leading indicator of demand weakness? $SPY $IWM
ITEMS.tweetB: Neil Sethi: Manufacturing new orders surge to 54.0, yet employment sub-index has been negative for 32 straight months. Factories filling orders without adding headcount. Structurally odd.

ITEMS.title: S&P PMI 55.1 — supply chains worst since Aug 2022
ITEMS.summary: S&P Global US Manufacturing PMI hit 55.1 for May. Sethi flags that the supply-chain stress sub-component has deteriorated to its worst reading since August 2022. Input prices are rising sharply, and lead times are lengthening. If sustained, this feeds directly into PPI and eventually CPI — complicating the Fed's path.
ITEMS.tweetA: S&P Global Manufacturing PMI: 55.1. Supply chain stress worst since Aug 2022. Input prices rising. Lead times lengthening. This is the 2021 inflation replay — just slower. Watch PPI. $TIP $SPY
ITEMS.tweetB: Neil Sethi: S&P PMI 55.1 but supply-chain sub-index just hit worst reading since August 2022. Input costs jumping. If this sticks, the Fed's inflation problem comes back before they've declared victory.

ITEMS.title: Global rates at 2-decade highs; debt is the structural engine
ITEMS.summary: The Credit Strategist argues that global sovereign yields at 20-year highs are not a temporary anomaly but the equilibrium for the next decade. The driver: debt servicing costs are now self-compounding. The US alone will spend $1.2T+ on interest in 2026, crowding out everything else and keeping the term premium structurally elevated.
ITEMS.tweetA: Global rates at 20-year highs aren't a bug — they're the new floor. Debt servicing is self-compounding. US pays $1.2T+ in interest in 2026. Term premium will stay elevated. Rethink duration. $TLT $TMF
ITEMS.tweetB: Credit Strategist: sovereign yields at 2-decade highs are equilibrium, not overshoot. US debt service at $1.2T+ crowds out all else and keeps long rates pinned high. This is a decade-long regime, not a cycle.

ITEMS.title: Fed RMPs = QE by another name ($40B/month T-bills)
ITEMS.summary: The Credit Strategist identifies the Fed's Repo Market Purchases (RMPs) as effectively quantitative easing in disguise. The Fed is purchasing ~$40B/month in T-bills via the reverse repo facility unwind — injecting liquidity while officially claiming QT. This, he argues, is suppressing volatility and enabling the equity rally.
ITEMS.tweetA: The Fed is running stealth QE: $40B/month in T-bill purchases via RMP facility. Officially it's 'QT.' Practically it's liquidity injection. This explains why vol stays crushed and equities keep grinding. $VIX $SPY
ITEMS.tweetB: Credit Strategist: Fed's Repo Market Purchases = $40B/month T-bill buying = QE relabeled. The reverse-repo unwind is re-liquefying markets while the official narrative says tightening. Don't trust the label.

ITEMS.title: 70% of US fixed income = T-bills; fiscal time bomb ticking
ITEMS.summary: A staggering 70% of US fixed income outstanding is now in T-bills (short duration). This means the government is acutely exposed to any rate spike — a massive refinancing wall hit every few months. The Credit Strategist frames this as a structural fragility: one inflation scare or demand strike at a Treasury auction could cascade rapidly.
ITEMS.tweetA: 70% of US fixed income is T-bills. The entire US debt stack reprices every few months. One bad Treasury auction or inflation scare = immediate fiscal pain. This is the most dangerous debt structure since WWII. $TLT
ITEMS.tweetB: Credit Strategist: 70% of US fixed income = T-bills. Every rate shock hits the entire debt stock almost instantly. This is not normal. It's a structural fragility that makes every Fed decision load-bearing.

ITEMS.title: 77.4% of planned US datacenter capacity only in spreadsheets
ITEMS.summary: Of all announced US datacenter builds, 77.4% exist only as planning documents or LOIs — not as permitted, funded, or under-construction projects. The Credit Strategist uses this to argue that the AI infrastructure narrative is running far ahead of physical reality. Power constraints, permitting delays, and capital allocation bottlenecks will cause a multi-year lag between announced and actual capacity.
ITEMS.tweetA: 77.4% of announced US datacenter capacity is PowerPoint, not concrete. Permitted & funded projects are a fraction. Power, permitting, and capex gaps mean the AI infra surge is 3-5 years from delivery. $EQIX $DLR $VRT
ITEMS.tweetB: Credit Strategist: only 22.6% of announced US datacenter capacity is real (permitted/funded). The rest is aspirational. The AI capex narrative is outrunning what can physically be built. Gap = multi-year.

ITEMS.title: SpaceX IPO = potential bubble zenith like Blackstone 2008
ITEMS.summary: The Credit Strategist draws an explicit parallel between the anticipated SpaceX IPO and the Blackstone IPO at the peak of the 2007 credit bubble. Both represent the moment when the hottest private asset of the cycle is finally offered to public investors — historically a contrarian sell signal for the broader market and the sector.
ITEMS.tweetA: SpaceX IPO as bubble zenith thesis: Credit Strategist compares it to Blackstone's 2007 IPO — peak of the credit cycle, hottest asset, maximum retail excitement. When the best private story goes public... that's the top. $SPY $ARKK
ITEMS.tweetB: Credit Strategist: SpaceX IPO could mark the bubble's zenith the way Blackstone IPO did in 2007. The hottest private asset finally accessible to retail at maximum valuation enthusiasm. Study the pattern.

ITEMS.title: Token demand 10x/year vs compute supply 3-4x = compute crunch nearing
ITEMS.summary: Epoch AI's analysis shows AI token demand is growing ~10x per year (driven by longer context windows, agentic chains, reasoning models). Compute supply is only growing 3-4x per year. The math produces an inevitable crunch point — they estimate 2027-2028 as when demand fully exhausts available supply, with pricing implications for GPU access.
ITEMS.tweetA: Epoch AI math: token demand growing 10x/year. Compute supply: 3-4x/year. The gap closes in 2027-2028. Compute crunch is a structural inevitability. Pricing power shifts entirely to GPU owners. $NVDA $AMD $AVGO
ITEMS.tweetB: Epoch AI: demand for AI compute growing 10x/yr; supply growing 3-4x/yr. That gap is not closable at current capex rates. By 2027-28, we hit the wall. Compute becomes the scarce resource in every AI business model.

ITEMS.title: Hyperscaler capex quadrupled since GPT-4; $770B 2026, $1T+ 2027
ITEMS.summary: Epoch AI documents that hyperscaler AI capital expenditure has quadrupled since GPT-4 launched in 2023. Projected 2026 spend is ~$770B; 2027 projections cross $1T for the first time. They note that this is now larger than the entire defense budgets of most nations, and is compressing the ROIC calculus for all hyperscalers.
ITEMS.tweetA: Hyperscaler AI capex: quadrupled since GPT-4. $770B in 2026. $1T+ in 2027. This is larger than most nations' defense budgets. And ROIC math is getting harder. $MSFT $GOOGL $AMZN $META
ITEMS.tweetB: Epoch AI: hyperscaler capex 4x since GPT-4. Heading to $770B (2026) → $1T+ (2027). The AI infrastructure bet is the largest single capital allocation in corporate history. ROIC implications are not yet priced.

ITEMS.title: RTX Spark: Edge Token Factory thesis — enterprise PC refresh supercycle
ITEMS.summary: BEP Research outlines a thesis around RTX Spark as the architecture enabling an enterprise PC refresh supercycle. The 'Edge Token Factory' concept: enterprise devices run local inference tokens at low latency, eliminating cloud round-trips for routine tasks. The catalyst is BYOD/corporate refresh cycles aligning with AI capability threshold crossings in 2026-2027.
ITEMS.tweetA: RTX Spark = Edge Token Factory. BEP Research thesis: enterprises refresh PCs to run local inference, cut cloud costs, and gain latency advantages. PC refresh supercycle starting 2026-2027. $NVDA $INTC $HPQ $DELL
ITEMS.tweetB: BEP Research: RTX Spark enables 'Edge Token Factories' — enterprise devices running local AI inference at near-zero latency. This catalyzes the biggest PC refresh cycle since laptops replaced desktops. 2026-27 timing.

ITEMS.title: US AI economy 2,600% quality-adjusted growth; $219B compute spend in 2025
ITEMS.summary: Import AI cites new analysis showing the US AI economy has grown 2,600% on a quality-adjusted basis since 2020. Nominal compute spend reached $219B in 2025. The quality-adjustment reflects exponential improvements in model capability per dollar — meaning the real AI economy is growing far faster than nominal numbers suggest.
ITEMS.tweetA: US AI economy: 2,600% quality-adjusted growth since 2020. $219B in compute spend in 2025. Nominal GDP and sector statistics massively understate what's actually happening in AI. The productivity unlock is invisible in traditional metrics.
ITEMS.tweetB: Import AI: quality-adjusted US AI economy +2,600% since 2020. $219B compute spend in 2025. The real AI economy is growing exponentially faster than headline numbers — because capability per dollar is compounding too.

ITEMS.title: UK AISI: automated alignment harder than expected
ITEMS.summary: The UK AI Safety Institute published findings that automated alignment approaches — using AI to help align AI — are proving harder than anticipated. Core issue: the evaluator models inherit biases from training data, making them unreliable as ground truth. This undermines the 'scalable oversight' assumption that many lab safety roadmaps depend on.
ITEMS.tweetA: UK AISI: automated alignment — using AI to align AI — is harder than expected. Evaluator models inherit training biases, can't serve as ground truth. This breaks the 'scalable oversight' assumption most lab safety roadmaps depend on.
ITEMS.tweetB: Import AI / UK AISI finding: automated alignment approaches aren't working as hoped. The evaluators have the same biases as the models they're evaluating. Scalable oversight — the fallback safety plan — may not scale.

ITEMS.title: Biohub ESMFold2 outperforms AlphaFold 3; cancer hit rates 36-88%
ITEMS.summary: Chan Zuckerberg Biohub released ESMFold2, which outperforms AlphaFold 3 on several protein structure prediction benchmarks. More significantly: when applied to cancer target proteins, the model identified drug binding sites at hit rates of 36-88% — dramatically above prior computational methods. This could compress drug discovery timelines by years.
ITEMS.tweetA: CZ Biohub ESMFold2 outperforms AlphaFold 3. Applied to cancer targets: drug binding hit rates of 36-88%. This isn't incremental — it's a step change in computational drug discovery. Timelines compress by years. $RXRX $SCHD
ITEMS.tweetB: Import AI: ESMFold2 (CZ Biohub) beats AlphaFold 3 on benchmarks, and more importantly — hits cancer drug binding sites 36-88% of the time computationally. That's extraordinary. Drug discovery is being disintermediated.

ITEMS.title: Australian politician calls to price extinction risk; 'survival capital'
ITEMS.summary: An Australian Member of Parliament made a formal speech calling for pricing AI extinction risk into financial markets via a new asset class he termed 'survival capital.' He argued that current markets have no mechanism to price civilizational-scale risks and that regulatory frameworks must address this. Import AI covers this as a sign of political mainstreaming of AI risk.
ITEMS.tweetA: An Australian MP just argued for a new asset class: 'survival capital' — instruments that price AI extinction risk. Markets have no mechanism for civilizational downside. Political mainstreaming of AI x-risk just happened.
ITEMS.tweetB: Import AI: Australian politician formally proposes 'survival capital' — financial instruments that price AI extinction risk. It's a fringe idea, but a sitting MP arguing for it in parliament is a signal of where this goes next.

ITEMS.title: AI revenues $25B/quarter; doubling every 0.73 years; Anthropic 80x growth
ITEMS.summary: Exponential View compiles AI revenue data showing the sector is generating ~$25B in quarterly revenues and doubling every 0.73 years (roughly every 9 months). Anthropic specifically has grown 80x in revenue terms over the past 24 months. Azeem Azhar argues this is the fastest revenue ramp of any technology in history, including the internet.
ITEMS.tweetA: AI revenues: $25B/quarter. Doubling every 0.73 years (~9 months). Anthropic: 80x revenue growth in 24 months. Exponential View calls this the fastest tech revenue ramp in history — faster than internet, mobile, cloud. $AMZN $MSFT $GOOGL
ITEMS.tweetB: Exponential View: AI sector revenues at $25B/quarter and compounding at 2x/9 months. Anthropic 80x in 2 years. Azeem Azhar: this is faster than anything we've seen — internet, mobile, cloud all included. The numbers are wild.

ITEMS.title: AI capex at 1% of US GDP — matches 1990s telecom buildout peak
ITEMS.summary: AI capital expenditure has now reached approximately 1% of US GDP — the same proportion as telecom infrastructure investment at the peak of the 1990s buildout. Exponential View uses this as context: the telecom overbuild preceded both spectacular value creation (internet economy) and massive capital destruction (dot-com bust). The question is which AI mirrors.
ITEMS.tweetA: AI capex = 1% of US GDP. That's exactly where telecom investment peaked in the 1990s buildout. What followed: massive value creation AND massive capital destruction. The question is which AI mirrors. $QQQ $IWF
ITEMS.tweetB: Exponential View: AI capex just hit 1% of US GDP — matching the 1990s telecom buildout peak exactly. That buildout created the internet economy AND destroyed trillions in capital. Study 1999-2003 for what happens next.

ITEMS.title: Dimon vs CLARITY Act; Lummis: next chance is 2030
ITEMS.summary: Jamie Dimon publicly opposed the CLARITY Act crypto framework in Congressional testimony, calling it premature and dangerous. Senator Lummis (pro-crypto) acknowledged that if the CLARITY Act fails to pass in the current Congress, the next realistic legislative window is 2030. This is a major setback for crypto regulatory clarity and the institutional adoption thesis.
ITEMS.tweetA: Dimon opposed the CLARITY Act in Congress. Lummis response: if it fails now, next window is 2030. Regulatory clarity for crypto just got pushed out 4 years. Institutional on-ramp thesis takes a serious hit. $BTC $ETH $COIN
ITEMS.tweetB: The News Block: Dimon kills CLARITY Act momentum in Congress. Lummis concedes next shot is 2030. US crypto regulation = 4-year gap. Offshore jurisdictions win. Coinbase and crypto-native infrastructure are repricing.

ITEMS.title: US seized $1B Iranian crypto (stablecoins); Iran pivots to Bitcoin
ITEMS.summary: The US Treasury and DOJ announced the seizure of approximately $1B in Iranian-controlled crypto assets, primarily stablecoins, held across multiple wallets. In response, Iranian state actors are reportedly pivoting to Bitcoin and privacy coins for sanctions evasion. This represents a new front in financial warfare and has implications for stablecoin regulation.
ITEMS.tweetA: $1B in Iranian crypto seized by US Treasury/DOJ — primarily stablecoins. Iran's response: pivot to Bitcoin and privacy coins. Stablecoins are now confirmed as seizable. The geopolitical case for non-custodial BTC is being written in real time. $BTC
ITEMS.tweetB: US seizes $1B in Iranian stablecoins. Iran pivots to Bitcoin. This is financial warfare going live on-chain. Stablecoins = confiscatable. BTC = less so. Every sanctioned state is watching this and updating their playbook.

ITEMS.title: Strive 16,500 BTC; SATA first daily dividend (13% annual, June 16)
ITEMS.summary: Strive Asset Management reported holding 16,500 BTC in its treasury. Separately, SATA (a Bitcoin yield product) announced its first daily dividend payment — equivalent to 13% annual yield — starting June 16. The News Block frames this as corporate treasury Bitcoin adoption continuing regardless of price action.
ITEMS.tweetA: Strive: 16,500 BTC in treasury. SATA announcing first daily dividend at 13% annual yield starting June 16. Corporate BTC treasury trend is alive and accelerating regardless of BTC price action. $MSTR $BTC $SATA
ITEMS.tweetB: The News Block: Strive now holds 16,500 BTC. SATA's first daily dividend = 13% annual yield, June 16 start. The institutional Bitcoin accumulation story doesn't need a bull market — it just needs a balance sheet.

ITEMS.title: Loracle lost $40M shorting HYPE; BTC outflows $1.67B; death cross
ITEMS.summary: On-chain analytics: Loracle (large DeFi trader) lost approximately $40M shorting HYPE token — one of the largest single on-chain losses in recent memory. Bitcoin spot ETFs recorded $1.67B in outflows over the past week. BTC technicals show a death cross forming (50-day MA crossing below 200-day MA) — historically a bearish signal though often a lagging indicator.
ITEMS.tweetA: Loracle lost $40M shorting $HYPE. BTC ETF outflows: $1.67B this week. Death cross forming on BTC chart. Crypto sentiment turning. The easy money on the long side has been made — positioning is shifting. $BTC $ETH
ITEMS.tweetB: Blocmates: Loracle liquidated for $40M on $HYPE short. BTC sees $1.67B ETF outflows. Death cross incoming. Three concurrent bearish signals in crypto. Not panic — but the tide is pulling back from the beach.

ITEMS.title: 4/4 risk-on signals; S&P 7,580 (+11.5% above 200-day); gold $4,600
ITEMS.summary: Lead Lag Report's proprietary risk dashboard shows all four risk indicators in risk-on territory. The S&P 500 is trading at 7,580 — 11.5% above its 200-day moving average, a historically stretched reading. Gold hit $4,600/oz. Lead Lag warns that while the signal is 'on,' the magnitude of the deviation from trend increases the probability of mean reversion.
ITEMS.tweetA: Lead Lag: 4/4 risk-on signals. S&P at 7,580 (+11.5% above 200-day MA). Gold at $4,600. Everything is on — which means the risk is how extended we are, not whether we're trending. Mean reversion math is building. $SPY $GLD
ITEMS.tweetB: Lead Lag Report: all four risk indicators green. But S&P is 11.5% above its 200-day — historically the top 5% of stretched readings. Gold at $4,600. Being risk-on while 11.5% extended is a different calculation than being risk-on at the mean.

ITEMS.title: Oil spiking 8% today; software rotation; IGV reclaims 200-day MA
ITEMS.summary: Intraday alert: oil up 8% on the session — attributed to the Iranian seizure news and crude draw data combining. Simultaneous sector rotation out of energy and into software; IGV (Software ETF) reclaimed its 200-day moving average for the first time since February. Bulltard frames this as a risk-on within-tech rotation, not macro deterioration.
ITEMS.tweetA: Oil +8% today (Iranian seizure + crude draw combo). But money rotating INTO software: IGV reclaims 200-day MA for first time since Feb. Risk-on rotation within tech. This is not a macro deterioration signal — it's sector positioning. $XLE $IGV
ITEMS.tweetB: James Bulltard intraday: oil +8% on Iran seizure + massive draw. Simultaneously, IGV (software) reclaims 200-day MA. Energy spikes but capital flows into tech. Market saying: energy is a trade, software is the trend.

ITEMS.title: Pentagon deploying drone swarms faster than it can sustain them
ITEMS.summary: a16z defense tech analysis: the Pentagon's drone procurement and deployment is outpacing its logistics and sustainment capacity. The supply chain for drone components (particularly batteries, cameras, and communications modules) is insufficient for the deployment tempo. a16z argues this creates a massive opportunity for defense tech startups in sustainment, logistics, and component manufacturing.
ITEMS.tweetA: a16z: Pentagon is deploying drone swarms faster than it can sustain them. Supply chain for batteries, sensors, comms modules is the bottleneck. Defense tech opportunity = sustainment and logistics, not just the drones themselves. $LMT $ANDURIL
ITEMS.tweetB: a16z defense: the DoD's drone deployment tempo has outrun its sustainment capacity. This creates the classic military logistics opportunity: not the weapon, but the supply chain behind it. The sustainment startups will be large companies.

ITEMS.title: SpaceX IPO = largest in history; wave: OpenAI, Anthropic, Databricks to follow
ITEMS.summary: ARK Invest analysis positions the SpaceX IPO as likely the largest IPO in US history by market cap at listing. More importantly, ARK argues it will open the floodgates for a wave of mega-IPOs: OpenAI, Anthropic, Databricks, and Stripe are all identified as waiting on SpaceX to go first. ARK is long the thesis and has been building positions in secondary market instruments.
ITEMS.tweetA: ARK: SpaceX IPO = largest in US history. Then: OpenAI, Anthropic, Databricks, Stripe. SpaceX going public opens the floodgates for the largest IPO wave since 1999. Public market investors get access to AI for the first time. $ARKK $ARKG
ITEMS.tweetB: ARK Invest: SpaceX = largest IPO in history, and the queue behind it — OpenAI, Anthropic, Databricks, Stripe — is the biggest private-to-public transfer of value since the dot-com era. This is the 1999 moment for AI.

ITEMS.title: ServiceNow at 5.6x NTM at April bottom; thesis confirmed
ITEMS.summary: Haymaker (EVA/economic value-added framework) revisits their April call on ServiceNow: at the April lows, NOW was trading at 5.6x NTM EVA — a historically cheap level for a franchise of its quality. Haymaker argues the subsequent recovery confirms the thesis and uses this to make the broader point that EVA-based valuation outperforms P/E in identifying turning points.
ITEMS.tweetA: Haymaker: ServiceNow hit 5.6x NTM EVA at the April bottom — historically cheap for a tier-1 software franchise. The recovery since confirms the EVA framework's edge in identifying turning points. NOW was the buy of the quarter. $NOW
ITEMS.tweetB: Haymaker EVA framework call: $NOW at 5.6x NTM EVA in April = screaming buy. The framework found the bottom when P/E valuations were still scary. EVA-based valuation identifies inflections that earnings multiples miss.

ITEMS.title: 18 of 20 BofA valuation measures flag S&P overbought
ITEMS.summary: Bank of America's equity strategy team reports that 18 of their 20 proprietary valuation metrics are now flashing 'overbought' or 'extended' signals on the S&P 500. This is the highest reading in the dataset's history. BofA does not make a directional call but notes that at prior peaks with 15+ signals, 12-month forward returns averaged -3.2%.
ITEMS.tweetA: BofA: 18 of 20 valuation metrics flagging S&P overbought — a record in their dataset. At prior peaks with 15+ flags, 12-month forward returns averaged -3.2%. Not a crash call — but the probability distribution has shifted. $SPY $SPX
ITEMS.tweetB: TheStreet/BofA: 18/20 valuation metrics at 'extended' on S&P. Highest ever in their data. Prior 15+ signal peaks: avg 12-month return = -3.2%. The market can stay irrational, but the risk/reward is now asymmetrically negative.

ITEMS.title: BTC closed May negative while equities hit records
ITEMS.summary: Ecoinometrics documents a historical anomaly: Bitcoin closed May 2026 in negative territory while US equities hit all-time records in the same month. Historically, BTC has tracked risk-on periods closely. This divergence suggests either BTC is undergoing a regime change (decoupling from equities risk-on) or it is building a coiled spring for a catch-up move. Ecoinometrics favors the latter.
ITEMS.tweetA: Bitcoin closed May negative. S&P hit all-time records in the same month. This has happened only twice before — both preceded major BTC catch-up moves within 60 days. Ecoinometrics: coiled spring, not decoupling. $BTC
ITEMS.tweetB: Ecoinometrics: BTC down in May while equities hit ATHs — a rare historical divergence. In prior instances, BTC violently caught up within 60 days. Not a decoupling signal — a compression signal. Watch June. $BTC $MSTR
